“When it comes to autos, we’re looking for some aspects within the production process to be considered to increase the American content and increase the deduction” under the existing tariff regime, Gutierrez said Thursday at Bloomberg Insights and Innovation Summit in Mexico City.

Read: Sheinbaum Touts Her Strong Ties to Trump to Sell CEOs on Mexico

Gutierrez predicted that the US-Mexico-Canada free trade agreement Trump signed in his first term would be strengthened but said Mexico wants to have a more effective dispute-resolution process established. He had previously said that the 2026 review process for the deal — known as the USMCA — may be sped up under Trump.

“We’re going to continue seeing tariffs in the coming years, and we will have to accustom ourselves to a model where the most important thing is not having a 0% tariff, but having the lowest tariff in the market, or in comparison to other countries. That’s what our country is managing to do each day,” he said.

item.title: Pimco Said to Back Paschi (BMPS) Bid for Mediobanca at Investor Meeting
item.description:
Pacific Investment Management Co is set to back Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena SpA ’s bid for rival Mediobanca SpA at a crucial investor meeting next week, people familiar with the matter said.

Pimco, which manages assets of around $2 trillion, plans to vote in favor of Monte Paschi’s proposal to issue the fresh shares it needs to finance the offer, the people said. The US firm controls a stake of around 1.5%, according to the people, who asked to not be named as the deliberations aren’t public.

The support from an influential shareholder is important for Monte Paschi Chief Executive Officer Luigi Lovaglio , who’s seeking to clear a major hurdle in his surprise bid to take over rival Mediobanca.

Paschi’s shareholders will convene April 17 to vote on whether to authorize Lovaglio to issue new shares, a step that would give him the needed currency for the deal while diluting existing shareholders.

Representatives for Pimco and Monte Paschi declined to comment.

Monte Paschi earlier this year surprised investors with an all-share bid to acquire Mediobanca.

Mediobanca CEO Alberto Nagel has rejected the bid as hostile, saying it lacks industrial and financial rationale and would destroy value.

Read More: Paschi CEO to Meet Investors in Milan as Firm Pursues Mediobanca

Monte Paschi has said a combination of the two firms would benefit from complementary platforms in asset gathering, private banking, investment banking and insurance.

Some analysts have expressed doubts about Monte Paschi’s plan. Brokers including KBW and Equita have issued notes citing lack of synergies, while Moody’s Ratings has highlighted potential risks for Mediobanca’s future creditworthiness. Proxy advisers have been mixed on the deal.

US investors Calvert, the New York City Comptroller and the Florida State Board of Administration , with stakes below 0.1%, have indicated they’ll vote against the proposal.

Monte Paschi needs approval from two-thirds of the shareholders attending the meeting to proceed with its plan.

The bank can count on the support of the Italian government — its largest investor — along with real estate mogul Francesco Gaetano Caltagirone and the Del Vecchio family holding Delfin Sarl, Bloomberg News has reported. Those investors combined own about 30% of the bank’s share capital.

Monte Paschi’s January announcement of its bid added to a whirlwind of proposed deals in Italy. Banco BPM SpA , the country’s third-biggest lender, has offered to buy asset manager Anima Holding SpA , only to become a takeover target itself when UniCredit SpA launched a bid for it a few weeks later. BPER Banca SpA has since offered to buy Banca Popolare di Sondrio SpA .

item.title: Goldman Lifts US Junk, Loan Default Forecasts on Tariff Woes
item.description:
Goldman Sachs Group Inc. strategists revised higher their forecasts for debt defaults by US junk bond issuers and leveraged-loan borrowers, citing widening credit spreads and pressures that US tariff policies put on the world’s largest economy.

The default rate forecast for US leveraged loan borrowers by the year-end was revised upward to 8% from 3.5%, according to an April 10 Goldman note from credit strategists led by Lotfi Karoui . They also raised the rate for US junk bond issuers to 5% from 3%.

“A new headwind for credit returns has also emerged in the form of a positive correlation between rates and spreads,” they wrote. For borrowers rated CCC, “the economics of refinancing look bleak given the recent move wider in spreads.”

US President Donald Trump’s ratcheting up of a trade war with China and upending of global commerce norms deepened selloffs in stocks and bonds Friday, underscoring how quickly investor relief from his 90-day pause in higher levies evaporated.

On April 3 — a day after Trump’s so-called “Liberation Day” — the extra yield investors demand to own the risky debt instead of Treasuries widened by the most since 2020. US junk yield premiums topped 450 basis points this week, according to a Bloomberg index .

Given slower earnings growth and higher borrowing costs anticipated, the strategists also raised their estimate of dollar debt that may be downgraded from investment-grade status to junk. The so-called “fallen angels” volume is estimated to rise to $75 billion, they said, up from the earlier forecast of $40 billion.

Nearly a quarter of bonds rated BBB- are on outlook negative by at least one of the three major rating agencies, they noted.

“This is the highest level ever outside of the global financial crisis and the 2020 Covid shock,” they said.

item.title: Trump Tariff War to Turn Into ‘Tariff Peace’ Soon, Hungary's Orban Says
item.description:
US President Donald Trump will probably reach tariff deals soon with countries around the world, Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban said, as he sought to play down the longer-term effects of the trade war.

“There will be a new balance, a new situation and this may take one or two months” as the US adjusts its tariff rates, Orban told state radio in an interview on Friday. “Once the agreements are sealed, we won’t be talking about a tariff war any more. We’ll be talking about a tariff peace.”

item.title: Romania Inflation Bolsters Central Bank Caution on Rate Cuts
item.description:
Romanian inflation eased slightly last month, as the central bank weighs an outlook clouded by domestic risks and global trade tensions before potentially resuming monetary easing.

Consumer prices rose at an annual rate of 4.9% in March compared with 5.02% in February, the statistics office in Bucharest said on Friday. That’s in line with the 4.9% median estimate in a Bloomberg survey of economists. Prices advanced 0.3% from the previous month.

Policymakers held borrowing costs at 6.5% this week for a fifth meeting, citing “heightened uncertainties” due to risks stemming from energy and food prices, as well as the potential impact on commodity prices of trade policies pursued by other countries.

Inflation in the Black Sea nation and the rate path have been complicated by the budget deficit that the government sees at 7% of economic output this year. Reaching that goal may yet require additional measures, such as tax increases, after the re-run of a presidential election next month.

Read More: Romania Presidential Vote Risks Political Shake-Up, Premier Says

The central bank expects price growth to ease this year to 3.8%, which would still exceed the 3.5% top end of its target range.

The inflation rate will likely be close to 5% by the end of 2025 because of higher energy prices, according to Raiffeisen Bank analysts Andreea Elena Draghia and Nicolae Covrig . Policymakers will probably only make one rate cut of 25 basis points in November due to high uncertainty both for the inflation and overall economic outlook, they said.

“We expect the central bank to remain cautious,” the analysts said.

item.title: EU Will Make Most of 90-Day Trump Tariff Pause, Donohoe Says
item.description:
The European Union will use the 90-day pause in US tariffs to find an alternative solution with President Donald Trump , according to the head of the Eurogroup.

Describing the hiatus as a “window of opportunity,” Paschal Donohoe , who leads the meetings of euro-area finance chiefs, said that Brussels will “see if we can identify a negotiated alternative to a path that will lead us all to a world of lower growth, higher inflation, to many risks to the progress that we’ve made in recent years.”

The US president has claimed the EU was formed to “screw” the US and has repeatedly highlighted that the bloc’s trade-in-goods surplus is evidence of an unfair relationship. The EU is the US’s largest trading partner.

“I think that our best response would be to focus on the European economy itself, focus on bringing competitiveness back to Europe,” Finance Minister Andrzej Domanski told reporters. “I believe we need a truly European answer I wouldn’t like to see a race to the bottom in terms of supporting industries.”

Similarly, Donohoe said that he remains “very, very confident that the economic foundations of the euro area are resilient and strong.”

item.title: UK Growth in Surprising Surge Ahead of Trump’s Tariff War
item.description:
Britain’s economy grew much faster than expected in February — before Donald Trump’s tariffs crusade brought turmoil to global markets and stoked recession fears.

Gross domestic product rose 0.5% after a stagnant January — itself revised up from a previously estimated 0.1% decline, the Office for National Statistics said Friday. It was well above the 0.1% gain predicted by economists, with all the main sectors posting increases.

While the figures provide some relief for the Labour government, the respite could be fleeting as the White House started to upend the global economic order with a slew of tariff announcements in March and April.

item.title: US Drone Firm Eyes Gap in Nigeria Exposed by Trump Aid Cutbacks
item.description:
A US medical drone company is talking with Nigeria to expand its service in Africa’s most populous nation, as gaps left by a freeze in US aid opens up opportunities for the private sector.

An expansion of Zipline International Inc ’s infrastructure is under discussion “to ensure accessibility and prompt delivery of essential medical goods such as blood, vaccines and medicines,” said Eniitan Tejuoso, an adviser to President Bola Tinubu on upgrading Nigeria’s health-care system.

The country, which last year received $790 million in aid from the US, has approved $200 million in the 2025 budget to help make up the shortfall and established programs to provide medication to 10 million people with HIV, tuberculosis and malaria, said Tejuoso.

US President Donald Trump froze aid to Africa after he returned to the White House in January, cutting off vital support that totaled $13.4 billion in 2024.

His order halted health-care programs across in some of the poorest nations on earth, leaving medication stranded in warehouses and millions of people cut off from lifesaving assistance, said Caitlin Burton, Zipline senior vice president.

Nigerian officials “want to put durable infrastructure in place that’s going to solve a lot of the problems that have persisted and compounded with the stoppage of aid from USAID,” she said. “Where they see gaps right now from USAID, they can transfer the responsibility to us.”


An employee loads a drone at the Zipline distribution center in Bayelsa State, Nigeria. Source: Zipline International Inc.

The San Francisco-based firm, founded in 2014, already operates in three of Nigeria’s 36 states and is looking to expand its footprint nationally.

The firm is working with partners to build a distribution hub and training facility in southern Cross River State, where it is already present, to deliver services further afield. Burton declined to disclose the value of the contracts under discussion.

The company also operates in Rwanda, Ghana, Ivory Coast and Kenya and is in talks to expand to two other African countries, Zipline said.


A medical drone returns to the Zipline distribution center in Bayelsa State, Nigeria. Source: Zipline International Inc.

Its hub in Cross River is planned as a self-sustaining public-private partnership with the state government and is scheduled to start in the third quarter, said Bong Duke, chief executive officer of the Cross River Planning Commission.

“In the near future there will be no need to depend on any external support,” Duke said. “With the withdrawal of USAID, we might have a little bit of a shock but we’re planning to ameliorate it. We don’t want to remain beggars,” he said.
